questions.question: Why is traffic steering usually more than a lowest-latency routing problem?
options: Because health quality, capacity, cost, and policy safety can all outweigh raw latency during real incidents.
Because latency never matters for user-facing systems.
Because anycast always chooses the optimal full application path automatically.
Because routing decisions should ignore origin state.
questions.explanation: Operationally useful routing balances several objectives rather than optimizing one number blindly.

questions.question: What is the main benefit of separating control-plane policy from data-plane routing decisions?
options: It lets POPs act quickly within bounded policy while keeping global rollout and governance manageable.
It removes the need for health signals at the edge.
It guarantees all POPs always have fresh policy instantly.
It makes per-tenant policies impossible.
questions.explanation: Good edge systems combine local responsiveness with controlled policy publication.

questions.question: Why are dampening or cooldown windows important in steering systems?
options: They reduce oscillation and keep routing changes from amplifying noisy health or latency signals.
They eliminate the need for failover capacity.
They force all routes to stay on the same region permanently.
They only matter when browser caching is enabled.
questions.explanation: Without dampening, steering can flap and create new instability.

questions.question: Which metric best exposes that some POPs are using stale routing policy?
options: Steering policy version skew by POP combined with divergent selected-region patterns.
Only total request volume across the company.
The number of configured TLS ciphers.
Object storage utilization in unrelated services.
questions.explanation: Version skew and path divergence reveal stale or inconsistent control-plane rollout.

questions.question: What is the main trade-off of allowing premium route classes to use different steering policy?
options: Better product fit and differentiated latency goals, but more complexity and possible shared-fleet risk.
Lower operational complexity for every tenant.
Perfect isolation with no extra guardrails.
No need for global capacity planning.
questions.explanation: Differentiated policies can be valuable, but they require stronger isolation and governance.

questions.question: If the lowest-latency region is nearing saturation, what is the strongest routing decision principle?
options: Include capacity and failure headroom in path selection so routing does not optimize into an outage.
Always keep sending traffic there because latency is the only user-visible metric.
Disable observability while rerouting.
Switch every tenant globally to the farthest region.
questions.explanation: The best path is not the shortest path if it creates overload and instability.

questions.question: Why should steering decisions be explainable per request or route class?
options: Because customers and operators need evidence for why traffic took a path, especially during incidents or cost disputes.
Because explainability replaces the need for failover testing.
Because routing never changes after launch.
Because only premium customers care about latency.
questions.explanation: Routing is product-visible behavior, so it needs transparency and debugging support.

questions.question: What hidden risk appears when local POP decisions use only local observations with no shared context?
options: Different POPs may react inconsistently to noisy signals and create chaotic regional traffic patterns.
All POPs will become too conservative automatically.
It guarantees lower transit cost everywhere.
It removes the need for policy rollout safety.
questions.explanation: Purely local decisions can fragment behavior and make incidents much harder to reason about.
